Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study whenever I felt like I could handle it, which wasn't often because my anxiety makes it hard to sit down and focus. Sometimes I'd binge-watch lecture videos for hours, other times I'd go days without touching anything. With 11 online courses, it felt impossible to organize anything properly, so I just kind of drifted through it all.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then something would come up with my extracurriculars or I'd just feel too overwhelmed to start. Deadlines would hit and suddenly I'd be cramming everything at once. The stress of that cycle was exhausting, but I couldn't seem to break out of it no matter how much I wanted to.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being real. I'd log in when I remembered an assignment was due or when I got a notification that made my stomach drop. Sometimes a peer from my support group would message me asking about something, and that would remind me to check. I rarely logged in just to casually browse or stay on top of things.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I tried to make it a habit, logging in every morning with my coffee. But then my anxiety would spike about something else entirely, and I'd avoid the LMS because even seeing my incomplete tasks stressed me out. It never became automatic for me like it seemed to be for other students.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I'm a visual learner and I could pause, rewind, and really absorb things at my own pace. I watched over 200 minutes of them, which surprised me when I think about it. The discussion posts were less helpful because participating made me anxious, but occasionally reading what others wrote helped clarify concepts.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They felt overwhelming and I never knew where to start with them. The discussion forums I mostly avoided too because posting felt like putting myself out there for judgment. I told myself I'd get to them later, but later rarely came unless someone from peer support nudged me.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to something I'd be tested on. Visual stuff like diagrams, videos, or interactive elements kept my attention. If something felt abstract or like busy work, my brain would just shut down and I'd start scrolling my phone instead.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like they existed in a different universe. Required ones had this weight of consequence hanging over them, even if I still procrastinated on them. Optional stuff? I'd think 'that looks interesting' and then never touch it because I was already drowning in the required work. The guilt was different too—less sharp for skipping optional things.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my laptop and study from there without being connected. I also used YouTube videos from other educators when I didn't understand something from class. My notes were handwritten because typing stresses me out, so a lot of my actual learning happened completely offline and invisible.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd sit with my printed notes and downloaded materials, working through problems by hand. None of that showed up anywhere. Or I'd be on a call with someone from peer support, talking through concepts for an hour, genuinely learning—but the LMS would just show I hadn't logged in. My exam scores reflected that hidden work though.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, and then that was it—I'd study from the local file for weeks. It felt safer somehow, like I wasn't being watched or tracked. Plus my internet isn't always reliable given my situation, so having things offline was practical. The LMS probably thought I watched something once and forgot about it.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods, especially mid-semester. My anxiety would get so bad that even opening my laptop felt impossible. I'd have panic attacks thinking about how behind I was, which made me fall further behind. During those times, the peer support intervention actually helped—having someone check in made me feel less alone in the struggle.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from extracurriculars or stressed about money stuff, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Confusion was a big one too—if I didn't understand something, I'd just close the tab instead of seeking help because asking felt too vulnerable. The fatigue made everything feel ten times harder than it probably was.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things. Or I'd scroll through materials without actually reading them, just going through the motions. It felt like I was doing something, but nothing was actually sticking. Those sessions were basically useless even though they'd show up as activity.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important and still avoid it because starting felt overwhelming. The anxiety would build up around it until it became this massive thing in my head. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure, but really I was just scared of failing. By the time I finally did it, I'd rush through and not do my best work.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd ignore everything until the deadline was breathing down my neck, then scramble to get something done. It wasn't healthy—my stress would spike, I'd barely sleep, and the quality suffered. But without that external pressure, I just couldn't make myself engage consistently.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it was a mixed bag. Positive feedback surprised me and made me feel like maybe I wasn't as hopeless as I thought. Critical feedback would sometimes send me into a spiral where I'd avoid that course entirely for a while. I needed the feedback but also dreaded it, which is exhausting to live with.
Q18: The structure felt loose enough that I could fall through the cracks, which I did. With so many courses and no strict schedule forcing me to show up, it was easy to drift. The peer support helped create some external structure, but the course itself didn't really push me to stay consistent. I needed more scaffolding than was provided.
Q19: The video content being available anytime was great for my schedule and learning style. But the lack of clear checkpoints or mandatory check-ins made it easy to disappear. Discussion requirements felt forced and anxiety-inducing rather than helpful. If there had been smaller, more frequent low-stakes activities, I might have stayed more consistently engaged.
Q20: I think that's probably someone like me on a good week—logging in, watching stuff, reading things, feeling like they're doing something. But when it comes to actually producing work, the anxiety kicks in and nothing gets submitted. Activity doesn't equal output when you're struggling with the pressure of being evaluated.
Q21: That's literally me. My exam score was 82 and I got a Merit despite barely submitting assignments and having terrible attendance. I was learning, just not through the LMS. Downloaded materials, peer discussions, my own notes—all invisible. The system couldn't see that I actually understood the content, just that I wasn't playing the game their way.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked things and for how long, but they can't show if you were actually present mentally. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with peers, the genuine understanding that happens away from the screen. They also can't capture the emotional experience—the anxiety, the avoidance, the breakthroughs that happen internally.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental energy into understanding it. For me, it's not about how often I log in or how many posts I make. It's about those moments when something clicks and I feel genuinely curious or accomplished. That can happen anywhere, not just on a learning platform.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was fighting against my own anxiety just to do the bare minimum. It can't show the peer support conversations that kept me from dropping out entirely. It doesn't capture the hours I spent studying offline or the genuine interest I had in the material despite my terrible engagement metrics. I'm more than my login frequency.
